text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

15.1	The United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat) is the focal point for sustainable urbanization and human settlements in the United Nations system. It supports Member States in the development of sustainable cities and human settlements through its normative and operational work at the global, regional, national and local levels. UN-Habitat leads and coordinates the monitoring of and reporting on global progress in the implementation of the New Urban Agenda and the achievement of Sustainable Development Goal 11. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolution 3327 (XXIX), by which the Assembly established the United Nations Habitat and Human Settlements Foundation, and resolution 32/162, by which the Assembly established the United Nations Centre for Human Settlements (Habitat). The Assembly, through its resolution 56/206, transformed the Centre into the United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat), effective 1 January 2002. The Assembly, through its resolution 73/239, established a new governance structure for the Programme, consisting of the universal UN-Habitat Assembly, the 36-member Executive Board and the Committee of Permanent Representatives. The UN-Habitat Assembly adopted resolutions providing further guidance on the work of UN-Habitat at its first and second sessions, held in May 2019 and June 2023, respectively.

15.2	The Programme’s support is aimed at developing the capacity at all levels of government and other key stakeholders to formulate and implement policies, plans and concrete activities for sustainable urban development. Such support is also provided through the implementation of projects under the regular programme of technical cooperation and the United Nations Development Account.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

15.3	UN-Habitat operates in a world that will continue to urbanize over the coming three decades. Approximately 90 per cent of urban growth is taking place in less developed regions, where unplanned growth is driving urban sprawl, and where lack of adequate and affordable housing exacerbates poverty and inequalities, which manifest in the rise of informal settlements, slums and homelessness. By contrast, many cities in other parts of the world are facing negative growth and shrinking populations. The global housing challenge is intensifying, with the demand for affordable and adequate housing outpacing supply in urban areas, further deepening inequality and vulnerability. Globally, more than 2.8 billion people live in inadequate housing, a figure that includes over 1.12 billion who reside in slums and informal settlements. Additionally, there are 304 million international migrants, most of whom live in urban areas, and 75.9 million internally displaced people.

15.4	The proposed programme plan for 2027 is guided by the UN-Habitat strategic plan for 2026–2029, which was approved by the UN-Habitat Assembly in May 2025. The proposed programme structure is informed by the strategic focus and means of implementation. The programme of work centres on access to adequate housing, land and basic services for all, including the transformation of informal settlements and slums to help drive sustainable urbanization. This focus responds directly to the scale and urgency of global housing shortages and recognizes adequate housing as central for addressing broader development challenges, including poverty, inequality and vulnerability to the adverse impacts of climate change.

15.5	The programme structure distinguishes between the substantive programmatic areas through which UN-Habitat contributes directly to development outcomes and the enabling areas that strengthen the policy, knowledge and partnership ecosystems required to achieve sustainable urban development at scale. This structure also responds to lessons learned from the implementation and midterm evaluation of the previous strategic plan,‍ which highlighted the need for greater strategic prioritization, clearer programmatic focus and improved coherence across the organization’s normative and operational activities.

15.6	Subprogramme 1, Housing, land, basic services and urban environment, advances the central focus of the strategic plan on access to adequate housing, land and basic services for all, including the transformation of informal settlements and slums. Through this subprogramme, UN-Habitat will support Member States and local authorities in expanding access to adequate housing and basic services, strengthening land governance and promoting environmentally sustainable urban development, thereby contributing directly to improved living conditions, inclusive prosperity and resilient cities and human settlements.

15.7	Subprogramme 2, Planning, finance policy, localization and governance, operationalizes the first two means of implementation identified in the strategic plan, namely, integrated urban and territorial planning, management, investment and finance, and participatory multilevel governance and localization of the Sustainable Development Goals. Through this subprogramme, UN-Habitat will strengthen policy, planning and governance frameworks that enable sustainable urbanization, support the development of integrated planning systems and sustainable urban finance mechanisms and advance participatory governance processes that accelerate the localization and implementation of the Goals.

15.8	Subprogramme 3, Knowledge, data, digitalization and capacity development, operationalizes the third means of implementation, with a focus on strengthening knowledge systems and institutional capacities to support evidence-based policymaking and effective implementation of sustainable urban development policies and programmes. Through this subprogramme, UN-Habitat will advance the generation, collection and analysis of urban data and knowledge, promote digital and smart city approaches that support improved urban management and strengthen the capacities of national and local governments and other stakeholders, including monitoring of Sustainable Development Goal 11 and the implementation of the New Urban Agenda.

15.9	Subprogramme 4, Partnerships, coalitions, advocacy and communication, a fourth means of implementation, recognizes that achieving impact at scale requires strengthened collaboration among Member States, local and regional governments, international organizations, civil society, academia and the private sector, among others. Through this subprogramme, UN-Habitat will promote strategic partnerships, facilitate global and regional coalitions for sustainable urban development and strengthen advocacy and communication on the importance of adequate housing, land and basic services as drivers of sustainable development. It will also leverage the organization’s convening role through global platforms and partnerships to mobilize action, share knowledge and enhance the visibility and impact of sustainable urban development within the broader multilateral system.

15.10	Taken together, the four subprogrammes provide a coherent and mutually reinforcing structure that aligns programmatic delivery, enabling policy frameworks, knowledge generation and partnerships in support of the strategic focus of the strategic plan. The structure ensures that UN-Habitat can effectively deliver normative guidance, technical cooperation and knowledge products while mobilizing partnerships and strengthening institutional capacities across levels of government and among stakeholders.

15.11	The programme of work of UN-Habitat is presented under the new structure, with subprogrammes 1 to 4 of the proposed programme plan for 2026 reorganized and integrated across the new subprogrammes with an annex reporting against programme performance for additional deliverables and results from the proposed programme plan for 2026 (see annex to part A below).

15.12	Across regions, subprogramme implementation will be tailored to diverse development contexts – with particular attention to countries in vulnerable and crisis-affected contexts, including least development countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States, as well as middle-income and economies in transition – using context-specific thematic entry points and delivery approaches that reflect varying priorities, capacities and pathways to impact under each subprogramme.

15.13	Cities are hotspots for environmental challenges, accounting for approximately 75 per cent of global energy consumption, 70 per cent of global greenhouse gas emissions and more than 70 per cent of resource use. In the Pact for the Future (General Assembly resolution 79/1), Member States reiterated their commitment to ensuring access for all to adequate, safe and affordable housing and supporting developing countries in planning and implementing just, safe, healthy, accessible, resilient and sustainable cities. In that context UN-Habitat will support national, regional and local governments in integrating urban and territorial planning, management, and investment and finance aspects and will support them in developing sustainable construction value chains and policies, supported by innovations and technology, that help to overcome inequalities and build resilience.

15.14	The deterioration in progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and implementing the Paris Agreement in the context of interrelated global crises calls for more transformative multilevel action. UN-Habitat will continue to fast-track integration between its normative and operational work, scaling up and accelerating the impact of the organization. UN-Habitat recognizes that rapid urban growth requires bold, forward-looking and collective action to shape cities in ways that address the most pressing urban challenges. As a global advocate for urban development, UN-Habitat will support countries in navigating complex housing issues, including by supporting the open-ended intergovernmental expert working group on adequate housing for all, by providing evidence-based policy advice, capacity development and technical support, and by driving urban programmes to address complex housing issues.

15.15	In line with its capacity-building strategy for the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the New Urban Agenda, UN-Habitat will continue to localize the Sustainable Development Goals and to respond to local challenges by identifying and targeting specific capacity gaps while raising awareness and building skills in transformative solutions. UN-Habitat will strengthen capacity development as a cross-cutting function across its operations, through the continued digitalization and dissemination of knowledge and tools, supported by strategic partnerships for innovation, knowledge and learning across practices.

15.16	In its catalytic role as a knowledge hub and partner convenor, UN-Habitat will carry out advocacy, communication and outreach activities and mobilize broad-based support for the implementation of the New Urban Agenda. It will operationalize stronger stakeholder engagement and collaboration with universities and centres of knowledge and research in order to continue to leverage advocacy and knowledge platforms, including the thirteenth session of the World Urban Forum, the Urban Agenda Platform, coalitions, global corporate events, regional ministerial conferences, the ministerial meeting on urbanization and climate change and the intersessional activities of the open-ended intergovernmental expert working group on adequate housing for all.

15.17	Furthermore, UN-Habitat will continue to monitor and report on global trends and provide evidence for policymaking, including through the enhanced use of technologies for data collection and analysis, data visualization and management of information. It will continue to expand the roll-out of the Global Urban Monitoring Framework and its support for Member States and other stakeholders in monitoring and reporting on the progress in the implementation of the New Urban Agenda and other global agendas and the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals.

15.18	Regarding cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, UN-Habitat will continue its close cooperation with international and regional organizations, such as the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, the World Bank, the European Union and regional development banks and explore deepened partnerships with relevant institutions from the global South. UN-Habitat will maintain its focus on partnerships with local authorities and their associations and city networks, grass-roots organizations, foundations, international financial institutions, the private sector and academia to promote participatory and multilevel governance and support municipal efforts in the localization of the Sustainable Development Goals and will continue to serve as the secretariat and co-Chair of the Local2030 coalition.

15.19	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UN-Habitat will continue to collaborate with the United Nations programmes and funds as well as specialized agencies, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the United Nations regional economic and social commissions, and strengthen its strategic inter-agency partnerships, building on the United Nations system-wide strategy on sustainable urban development and agency-specific collaborative frameworks. Additionally, UN-Habitat will continue to advance the goals of the United Nations reform aimed at increasing sustainable development impacts at the country level with a focus on regional collaboration achieved through the regional development cooperation platforms and issue-based coalitions. UN-Habitat will also deepen its collaboration with the Development Coordination Office to strengthen the United Nations system-wide impact on sustainable development by providing technical guidance, contributing to the revision of system-wide guidance, helping to build capacity for the use of innovative diagnostic and planning methodologies, and working with the resident coordinator system and United Nations country teams to mainstream sustainable urbanization across common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks.

15.20	To pursue stronger integration of environmental, social and economic sustainability and the protection of biodiversity into urban development, UN-Habitat will continue to strengthen its collaboration with the United Nations Environment Programme through the Greener Cities Partnership, in serving as the secretariat for the Secretary-General’s Advisory Board of Eminent Persons on Zero Waste and as a partner in the Global Alliance for Buildings and Construction, which is led by the United Nations Environment Programme. UN-Habitat will also lead a new Issue Management Group on Urbanization and the Environment as part of the Environment Management Group; strengthen collaboration with the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for Road Safety; and continue to collaborate with the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, the United Nations Development Programme, the International Organization for Migration, the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction, the World Food Programme and all relevant United Nations entities and international actors to integrate sustainable urban development into resilience, humanitarian response, solutions to forced displacement and urban recovery.
15.21	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Adequate unearmarked extrabudgetary resources are available to implement the planned work and produce the deliverables;
(b)	Countries remain committed to the New Urban Agenda and the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development;

(c)	National, regional and local governments continue to request policy advice and technical assistance from UN-Habitat in the development and implementation of their urban legislation, policies, plans and strategies;
(d)	Urban indicators, data and information are used to formulate policies, with adequate mechanisms available for impact monitoring and performance assessments;
(e)	Countries in which UN-Habitat delivers large programmes maintain political, economic and social conditions that allow for the implementation of programmes and projects.

15.22	UN-Habitat will continue to mainstream social inclusion to address cross-cutting issues affecting women, older persons, persons with disabilities, and children and young people. UN-Habitat will also continue to implement and participate in mechanisms that strengthen the meaningful participation of young people in sustainable urban development, such as the UN-Habitat Youth Advisory Board and the Global Alliance Cities4Children. Furthermore, it will develop critical programmes to promote urban liveability, such as the Youth 2030 Cities programme and the Young Gamechangers initiative.

15.23	UN-Habitat integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, through its gender policy and action plan. Furthermore, the Advisory Group on Gender Issues advises the Executive Director on how to promote gender equality and women’s empowerment in sustainable urban development. UN-Habitat, in collaboration with the United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN-Women), will continue to support Member States with policy, legislation, planning and financing instruments to achieve integrated, inclusive and participatory results. For example, UN-Habitat will continue to promote land and housing policies that incorporate a gender perspective and improve tenure security for all, especially women.

15.24	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, UN-Habitat will continue to work with its partners, including the International Disability Alliance, the Global Disability Fund, World Blind Union, World Enabled and the Global Disability Innovation Hub, to provide support on disability-inclusive urban development. It will develop a dedicated action plan to mainstream disability inclusion across its programmatic work and internal operations, highlighting best practices while identifying critical needs in capacity development and technical assistance. This plan will supplement the established environmental and social safeguards applied to all projects.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

15.25	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative; in particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

15.26	UN-Habitat will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.

children.text: Evaluation activities

15.29	Five project and programme evaluations conducted by UN-Habitat relating to the programme cooperation agreement between UN-Habitat and the Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (for the period 2020–2025), 12 sessions of the World Urban Forum and other topics completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

15.30	In response to the results of the above-mentioned evaluations, UN-Habitat has incorporated findings and recommendations from the evaluation of the 12 sessions of the World Urban Forum to feed into the development of the thirteenth session, to be held in 2026 in Baku. The adjustments considered for the thirteenth session of the World Urban Forum include improvements of the venue and logistics, optimization of the World Urban Forum format, size and focus, as well as maximization of the networking and interaction potential of the event, all of which have been considered in the planning of the 2026 session. Moreover, the evaluation of the Programme Cooperation Agreement with the Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency highlighted the need for local ownership and the adaptation of interventions to the local context, which has informed the design of new interventions where UN-Habitat country offices have taken a leading role.
15.31	The following evaluations to be conducted by UN-Habitat are planned for 2027:
(a)	A midterm evaluation of the UN-Habitat strategic plan for 2026–2029;
(b)	One country programme evaluation;
(c)	One thematic evaluation on gender equality;
(d)	19 project and programme evaluations.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Housing, land, basic services and urban environment
Objective

15.32	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure access to adequate housing, land and basic services for all, transforming informal settlements and slums with access to clean, safe, affordable and equitable basic services that lead to the creation of low-carbon, resilient, inclusive, healthy and economically vibrant cities and neighbourhoods.
Strategy

15.33	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will support Member States in developing solutions to expand access to adequate housing, land and basic services (water, sanitation, mobility, waste management and energy) and to promote climate change adaptation and resilience, urban low-emission development strategies, accessibility and sustainability across the rural-urban continuum, with an emphasis on transforming informal settlements, slums and underserved areas, as well as in crisis-affected contexts. Specifically, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide technical assistance to Member States in the aforementioned areas;
(b)	Provide policy advisory services and advocacy in the aforementioned areas;
(c)	Develop standards, tools and guidelines for local and national governments;
(d)	Conduct research to inform decision-making on affordable housing and equitable access to basic services;
(e)	Provide capacity development and institutional support, including the dissemination of learning and best practices.
15.34	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in making progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goals 1, 3, 5–8, 10–11, 13 and 16.
15.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Increased access to adequate housing for all, benefiting people in cities and human settlements, particularly those in the poorest and those in vulnerable situations, who are often disproportionally affected by global challenges, such as housing shortages and climate change;

(b)	Improved and more equitable access to basic services, with a focus on low-carbon and climate-resilient urban basic services, particularly for people living in informal settlements and slums, as well as those living in crisis-affected contexts;

(c)	Integrated land-use planning and management that supports the social and ecological function of land, with a focus on increasing climate-resilient urban development, especially for the urban poor;
(d)	More efficient and equitable use of land, including strengthened security of tenure, benefiting communities and households, with a special emphasis on those in vulnerable situations.
Programme performance in 2025
Integrated sustainable mobility and transit‑supportive housing to expand equitable access to safe, equitable public transport in cities, particularly city peripheries

15.36	To support access to public transport, especially in city peripheries, which, owing to urban sprawl, is often where low- and middle-income households reside, the subprogramme supported Member States in integrating sustainable mobility and transit-supportive housing to expand access to livelihoods, education and essential services. Sustainable mobility initiatives were implemented in 29 cities across 21 countries, advancing safer, more inclusive and climate-responsive transport systems. Concrete implementation projects – focused on improving pedestrian and cycling infrastructure around public transport corridors and school zones – were carried out in Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria and Uganda, expanding access to safe, equitable public transport to 816,000 people. This work also contributed to the roll-out of the Pan‑African Action Plan for Active Mobility in five countries and supported the expansion of activities to more than 50 cities under the Global Alliance of Cities for Road Safety.
15.37	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 15.I).

15.38	The subprogramme’s work contributed to two additional partner cities (Cancún in Mexico and Trinidad in Cuba) and two additional countries (Costa Rica and the Comoros) for a total of 16 cities and 52 countries, which met the planned target for countries and exceeded the planned target for partner cities.

15.40	The subprogramme’s work contributed to increased security of tenure through the issuance of land ownership certificates in two additional cities (Gadhawa and Lamahi municipalities in Dang district, Nepal), for a total of 42 cities, which met the planned target.
15.41	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 15.III).
Figure 15.III
Performance measure: number of partner cities implementing innovative strategies, tools or approaches in order to increase the security of tenure rights to land (cumulative)
Result 3: increased access to adequate housing, land and basic services – Proposed programme plan for 2027

15.42	More than 2.8 billion people around the world face some form of housing inadequacy, including 1.1 billion people in informal settlements and slums, which underscores the need to ensure adequate housing and secure land tenure and basic services for all. The subprogramme provides technical assistance, standards and tools, policy dialogue and capacity-building to Member States to ensure adequate housing, strengthen urban policies and plans, improve land administration and tenure security, transform informal settlements and expand access to basic services, contributing to low-carbon and resilient cities and neighbourhoods.
Lessons learned and planned change

15.43	The lesson for the subprogramme was that adopting approaches that recognize and reinforce the interlinkages between housing, land and basic services can provide for synergies and complementarities in implementation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will position housing as a driver of sustainable urban development requiring a systems approach that embeds housing in broader socioeconomic and environmental strategies, empowering cities to become inclusive, connected and resilient for all.

Subprogramme 2 – Planning, finance policy, localization and governance
Objective

15.46	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance integrated urban planning, financing mechanisms, evidence-based policy frameworks, effective legislation and participatory multilevel governance systems for equitable access to adequate housing, land and basic services, taking into consideration the localization of the Sustainable Development Goals and mainstreaming gender equality and women’s empowerment.
Strategy
15.47	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide technical assistance and capacity development to Member States in integrated and gender-sensitive spatial planning, municipal finance, urban policy formulation, legal reforms, Sustainable Development Goals localization and participatory multilevel governance;

(b)	Facilitate knowledge exchange, peer learning and multi-stakeholder dialogue platforms that connect national, subnational and local governments with civil society, the private sector and development partners;

(c)	Support the development and implementation of gender-sensitive, normative frameworks, tools and good practice guidance on urban planning systems, innovative financing mechanisms, policy and legislative reforms and participatory multilevel governance;

(d)	Strengthen monitoring, evaluation and evidence generation on urban development trends, financing flows, policy and legislation effectiveness and multilevel governance outcomes to inform decision-making.
15.48	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in making progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goals 5, 10–11 and 16–17.
15.49	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	National and local institutions developing and implementing integrated and gender-sensitive urban planning systems, mobilizing domestic resources, managing public expenditure and coordinating multilevel governance for sustainable urban development;
(b)	Adoption and implementation of gender-sensitive urban policies, legal frameworks and financing mechanisms aligned with the New Urban Agenda and national development priorities;
(c)	Increased engagement and ownership by local governments, communities, women, young people and marginalized groups in participatory urban planning, budgeting and decision-making processes;
(d)	Strengthened alignment between national Sustainable Development Goal targets and local action, with improved tracking and reporting on urban-related indicators at subnational levels.
Programme performance in 2025
Planning law reforms strengthen multilevel governance for resilient and sustainable cities

15.50	Rapid urbanization and climate change are placing growing pressure on cities, exacerbating fragmented planning systems, unclear institutional mandates and weak coordination between national and local authorities. Addressing these challenges is essential to ensure that climate and urban development commitments translate into effective local action. The subprogramme supported Member States through analysis of legal and institutional frameworks, capacity development and comparative research to strengthen urban legislation as a foundation for resilient and sustainable development, focusing on clarifying institutional roles and responsibilities, improving legal coherence and reinforcing multilevel governance. For example, in 2025, stakeholder consultations and a UN-Habitat review of the Zimbabwe Climate Change Management Bill were undertaken to enhance national ownership of the proposed law and ensure broader institutional and community engagement in its implementation. Moreover, in Sierra Leone, the enactment of the Town and Country Planning Act (2025) strengthened decentralized urban governance by empowering local councils to lead spatial planning and development control, enhancing vertical coordination and integrated territorial planning. Also in 2025, Botswana initiated reforms to the 2013 Planning Law, informed by legal gap analyses, international comparative case studies and inclusive stakeholder consultations, modernizing the planning framework with effective governance mechanisms.
15.51	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 15.V).
Figure 15.V
Performance measure: number of countries undertaking urban legal and governance reforms (cumulative)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: upgraded own-source revenue systems to improve the performance of local governments – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

15.52	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 10 additional local governments, for a total of 40, upgrading their own-source revenue systems to improve revenue collection. For instance, rapid own-source revenue diagnostics were conducted in three Costa Rican municipalities (Palmares, Sarchí and Atenas), identifying gaps in registration, collection and valuation and providing targeted recommendations to improve revenue performance, which met the planned target.
15.53	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 15.VI).
Figure 15.VI
Performance measure: number of local governments that have developed strategies and reform initiatives to enhance own-source revenue (cumulative)

Result 2: Sustainable Development Goals localization fosters system change and territorial transformation by nurturing inclusive multilevel governance, stakeholder participation, institutional capacities and finance for local solutions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

15.54	The subprogramme advances system change and territorial transformation through Sustainable Development Goals localization and multilevel governance, translating global goals and national policies into local action. It supports sustainable urban development by strengthening institutional capacity, mobilizing partners and stakeholders, leveraging finance for local solutions and building the capacities of the United Nations System on Goals localization.
Lessons learned and planned change

15.55	The lesson for the subprogramme was that there were further opportunities to advance territorial transformation and the translation of global goals and national policies into local plans and impact through localization and multilevel governance. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will enhance its guidance and toolkits for Sustainable Development Goals localization, targeting the United Nations System and its partners, to demonstrate in practical terms how to translate global goals into local tangible outcomes. The subprogramme will consolidate technical assistance efforts into integrated support packages and facilitate the use of common methodologies, shared data platforms and coordinated capacity-development approaches. The subprogramme will promote regular knowledge-sharing across regional and global partners and will align its work more systematically with United Nations country teams to ensure coherent system-wide delivery.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Knowledge, data, digitalization and capacity development
Objective

15.58	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance inclusive, evidence-based and digitally enabled knowledge systems to ensure access to adequate housing, land and basic services.
Strategy
15.59	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Generate, synthesize and disseminate policy-relevant knowledge, analytical products and good practices on housing, land and basic services, including through research, global assessments, practical learning tools and policy papers, to support evidence-based decision-making by Member States and stakeholders;

(b)	Strengthen the collection, harmonization, analysis and use of data and indicators on housing, land and basic services at the global, national and subnational levels, including through the development of monitoring frameworks and tools aligned with international standards, the Sustainable Development Goals and the New Urban Agenda;

(c)	Promote the use of digital solutions and frontier technologies to improve data availability, accessibility and transparency and support innovative approaches, platforms and strategies for planning, monitoring and delivery of housing, land and basic services;

(d)	Build institutional and technical capacities of Member States and other stakeholders for the formulation, implementation and monitoring of inclusive housing, land and basic services policies and programmes, including through higher and professional education, training and digital learning, as well as peer learning and co-creation.
15.60	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in making progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goals 1, 4, 6, 9,11 and 17.
15.61	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased access to quality knowledge and data to inform housing, land and basic services policies and practice at the global, national and subnational levels;
(b)	Increased adoption of digital and innovative approaches to policymaking, implementation and monitoring related to housing, land and basic services;

(c)	Strengthened expertise and institutional capacities of Member States, at all levels, and of stakeholders to design, implement and monitor inclusive and equitable policies for housing, land and basic services.
Programme performance in 2025
Urban practitioners have open access to practical knowledge and guidance in multiple languages to help accelerate the implementation of the New Urban Agenda and the Sustainable Development Goals

15.62	Building the capacities of urban practitioners across the globe with adequate skills, knowledge and tools can be supported by developing and scaling digital learning solutions. The subprogramme leverages the potential of knowledge digitalization to enhance outreach and accessibility of learning on critical skills and capabilities. Starting in 2020, UN-Habitat piloted the creation of its first self-paced online courses based on the New Urban Agenda illustrated handbook. This marked the creation of the UN-Habitat Learn platform and the development of an instructional design function at UN-Habitat. The UN-Habitat Learn platform has helped with both scaling up and localizing the organization’s practical knowledge and guidance, with more than 25 different courses to date, providing practical guidance to advance the New Urban Agenda and the Goals, free and accessible to all. The platform offers content in eight languages over 126 hours of learning. It also provides space for digital classrooms supporting hybrid or in-person training programmes. The platform’s course offering presents guidance and methodologies illustrating the main findings of normative and operational work, often capitalizing on multi-year, multi-partner and multi-country initiatives. This knowledge is integrated into learning pathways through an iterative co-creation process across UN-Habitat teams. In some cases, courses have been translated into non-official languages for further replication at the local level.
15.63	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 15.VII).
Figure 15.VII
Performance measure: number of students and practitioners enrolled in the UN-Habitat Learn platform and awarded certificates for completion of self-paced online courses (cumulative)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: digital transformation for sustainable urban development – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

15.64	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the development and continued dissemination of the UN‑Habitat people‑centred smart cities approach and associated operational toolkits, which did not meet the planned target of the endorsement by the UN-Habitat Assembly of the international guidelines on people-centred smart cities. children.text: Result 2: national and local authorities increase and improve the production of disaggregated and policy-relevant data on housing, land and basic services (cumulative) – Proposed programme plan for 2027

15.66	The subprogramme will build on previous work in knowledge generation, data, digital innovation and capacity development towards strengthening national and subnational capacities for Sustainable Development Goals and New Urban Agenda data collection, integration and reporting, expanding the use of geospatial and digital tools and enhancing data-to-policy linkages through targeted analytical products and capacity-building initiatives, in collaboration with national statistical systems and the Global Urban Data Coalition.
Lessons learned and planned change

15.67	The lesson for the subprogramme is that, while progress has been made in expanding urban data availability and digital tools, there are opportunities to further strengthen the institutionalization, interoperability and disaggregation of data and databases to help ensure impact on policy development. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on embedding housing, land and basic services data within national and subnational planning and reporting systems, strengthening coordination across institutions and data sources, and prioritizing equity-focused, disaggregated data approaches aligned with Sustainable Development Goals and New Urban Agenda monitoring and reporting requirements.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Partnerships, coalitions, advocacy and communication
Objective
15.70	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance cooperation among relevant stakeholders to ensure adequate housing, land and basic services for all.
Strategy
15.71	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Build and nurture inclusive partnerships and muti-stakeholder coalitions to support coordinated approaches on housing, land and basic services;

(b)	Convene global, regional and national advocacy platforms and facilitate stakeholder engagement, including through the World Urban Forum, World Habitat Day, World Cities Day (in the framework of the Urban October);
(c)	Engage in advocacy and strategic communication, outreach and knowledge-sharing to raise awareness and engagement in policy action for adequate housing, land and basic services for all.
15.72	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in making progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goals 1, 6, 10, 11 and 17.
15.73	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Strategic coalitions and cooperation supporting the implementation of solutions for housing, land and basic services at the global, national and local levels;
(b)	Improved visibility and accessibility of solutions for housing, land and basic services.
Programme performance in 2025
The Global Stakeholder Forum advances inclusive and accountable urban development

15.74	The subprogramme supports multi-stakeholder participation in UN-Habitat normative and intergovernmental work through the Global Stakeholder Forum as a structured platform for coordinated dialogue and consolidated stakeholder inputs, including in the lead-up to intergovernmental meetings. The subprogramme provided substantive, technical and organizational support to convene the Global Stakeholder Forum, facilitating broad-based participation from civil society, local authorities, the private sector and other partners. It coordinated stakeholder consultations, provided assistance to help consolidate key messages and recommendations and helped to ensure that these inputs were systematically channelled into UN-Habitat intergovernmental processes and normative work.

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 3: the World Urban Forum is leveraged to advance global commitments to ensure adequate housing, land and basic services for all – Proposed programme plan for 2027

15.76	Accelerating progress on sustainable urban development benefits from strengthened global advocacy, inclusive convening and coordinated multi-stakeholder engagement. The subprogramme has reinforced the World Urban Forum as a key platform for global dialogue and stakeholder mobilization. These efforts have helped to enhance the visibility of adequate housing and sustainable urbanization priorities and solutions, strengthening collective commitment and action towards the New Urban Agenda and the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 11.
Lessons learned and planned change

15.77	The lesson for the subprogramme was the possibilities to further strengthen and transform broad stakeholder participation in flagship events into more structured and inclusive engagement for actionable commitments and follow-up. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen mechanisms to capture and track stakeholder contributions and improve strategic communications to ensure that advocacy outcomes support implementation at all levels.
